steps.title: A local weighted sum
steps.body: Discrete convolution builds each output sample by sliding a small kernel across the input signal, multiplying overlapping entries, and summing the products.

steps.title: The index rule
steps.prompt: For $y[n]=\sum_k x[k]h[n-k]$, which input samples affect $y[n]$?
choices: Only samples where the shifted kernel overlaps the signal.
Every sample equally, no matter where the kernel is.
explanations: Correct. The kernel selects a local neighborhood and weights those samples.
No. The kernel has local support in this toy example.
No. Convolution is a weighted sum, not a max operation.

steps.title: Smoothing
steps.prompt: What does the kernel $[0.25,0.5,0.25]$ tend to do?
choices: It averages nearby values, so sharp changes are softened.
It reverses time without changing amplitudes.
explanations: Correct. It is a small smoothing kernel.
No. Positive weights do not isolate negative values.
Not here. The output is a local weighted sum.
